organic-cotton.low: Organic cotton at low construction stays clean and cost-stable — minimal seam work lets the fabric breathe and keeps lead time predictable.
organic-cotton.moderate: Organic cotton handles moderate construction well — structured seaming is achievable without inflating assembly time or risking drape loss.
organic-cotton.high: Organic cotton at high construction risks seam distortion under heavy finishing detail — sampling must validate that the hand doesn't fight the structure.

conventional-cotton.low: Conventional cotton at low construction is the most reliable execution path — stable, repeatable, and forgiving on timeline.
conventional-cotton.moderate: Conventional cotton supports moderate construction cleanly — it holds seam work without special handling and keeps SMV in a predictable range.
conventional-cotton.high: Conventional cotton at high construction introduces finishing sensitivity — the fabric holds structure but detailed seam work requires disciplined pressing and QC.

tencel.low: Tencel at low construction lets the drape do the work — minimal seaming preserves the fluid hand and keeps assembly demand low.
tencel.moderate: Tencel at moderate construction requires careful seam allowance — the fabric's drape can be disrupted by aggressive finishing, so construction detail must be intentional.
tencel.high: Tencel at high construction is technically demanding — the fluid hand fights structured seam work, and finishing intensity risks permanently altering the drape behavior.

linen.low: Linen at low construction is straightforward — the fabric's natural texture carries the aesthetic without needing construction complexity.
linen.moderate: Linen at moderate construction works well for structured pieces — it holds seam lines cleanly and tolerates finishing detail without significant handling risk.
linen.high: Linen at high construction requires careful edge finishing — the fabric's natural weave is prone to fraying under heavy seam detail, and sampling must validate that edge treatment holds before committing to production.

silk.low: Silk at low construction is the correct pairing — minimal seaming protects the hand and keeps slippage and handling time manageable.
silk.moderate: Silk at moderate construction is achievable but requires specialist handling — seam allowances must be generous and finishing must be minimal to protect the surface.
silk.high: Silk at high construction is a high-risk combination — the fabric's handling sensitivity compounds with complex seam work, driving SMV and sampling cycles significantly.

wool-merino.low: Merino at low construction under-uses the fabric's structure potential — it works, but the material supports more complexity without significant cost penalty.
wool-merino.moderate: Merino at moderate construction is the optimal pairing — the fabric holds seam work cleanly, finishing detail reads well, and the hand stays intact tembly.
wool-merino.high: Merino at high construction is technically viable but weight-sensitive — structured seam work is achievable, but finishing intensity must account for the fabric's natural loft and recovery.

cashmere-blend.low: Cashmere blend at low construction protects the hand and keeps assembly cost aligned with the material investment.
cashmere-blend.moderate: Cashmere blend handles moderate construction well when seam work is clean — the blend's structure supports finishing detail without compromising softness.
cashmere-blend.high: Cashmere blend at high construction is a premium commitment — assembly SMV climbs significantly and any finishing error is costly to correct given the material's price point.

recycled-polyester.low: Recycled polyester at low construction is cost-efficient and timeline-stable — the right call when margin and delivery are the binding constraints.
recycled-polyester.moderate: Recycled polyester handles moderate construction without issue — it's lly stable, holds seam work cleanly, and tolerates finishing detail well.
recycled-polyester.high: Recycled polyester at high construction is technically clean but aesthetically limiting — the fabric holds structure, but the synthetic hand under heavy finishing can read as over-engineered.

virgin-polyester.low: Virgin polyester at low construction is the most efficient execution path — stable, fast, and predictable across all handling stages.
virgin-polyester.moderate: Virgin polyester supports moderate construction reliably — dimensional stability makes seam work consistent and finishing detail holds without special handling.
virgin-polyester.high: Virgin polyester at high construction delivers structural precision — the fabric's stability is an asset at this complexity tier, though surface finish under heavy pressing needs QC attention.

nylon.low: Nylon at low construction keeps the build fast and abrasion-resistant — appropriate when durability and timeline are the priority.
nylon.moderate: Nylon handles moderate construction well — the fabric's strength supports seam work without handling risk, and lead time stays predictable.
nylon.high: Nylon at high construction introduces heat sensitivity at finishing stages — structured seam work is achievable, but pressing and finishing protocols must be tightly controlled.

leather.low: Genuine leather at low construction is the correct approach for most applications — minimal seaming protects the surface and keeps assembly time aligned with the material cost.
leather.moderate: Genuine leather at moderate construction requires specialist assembly — seam allowances must be glued before stitching and finishing detail demands experienced handlers.
leather.high: Genuine leather at high construction is a significant production commitment — SMV is high, rework is costly, and the sampling cycle must be longer than standard to validate every seam treatment.

vegan-leather.low: PU leather at lotion is straightforward — it handles like woven fabric at this complexity tier and keeps assembly cost low.
vegan-leather.moderate: PU leather supports moderate construction cleanly — it holds seam work without specialist handling and tolerates standard finishing protocols.
vegan-leather.high: PU leather at high construction risks delamination at seam edges under heavy finishing pressure — sampling must validate edge treatment and pressing protocols before committing.

rayon-viscose.low: Rayon at low construction lets the drape lead — minimal seaming preserves the fluid hand and keeps handling time low.
rayon-viscose.moderate: Rayon at moderate construction requires careful handling — the fabric's drape and slippage sensitivity mean seam allowances need to be generous and finishing controlled.
rayon-viscose.high: Rayon at high construction is a high-handling combination — slippage under complex seam work and sensitivity to finishing intensity make this a demanding production path.

deadstock-fabric.low: Deadstock at low construction is the right call — it maximises yardage efficiency and minimises cutting waste on a fixed-quantity material.
deadstock-fabric.moderate: Deadstock at moderate construction is workable if yardage allows — seam allowance planning must account for the fixed lot size with no reorder option.
deadstock-fabric.high: Deadstock at high construction is a risk — complex seam work increases cutting waste and any sampling error cannot be recovered with additional yardage.

hemp.low: Hemp at low construction is straightforward — the fabric's natural texture carries the aesthetic without requiring construction complexity.
hemp.moderate: Hemp handles moderate construction well — it's dimensionally stable, holds seam lines cleanly, and softens through assembly without losing structure.
hemp.high: Hemp at high construction is technically viable — the fabric's strength supports finishing detail, but surface texture under heavy pressing needs QC validation.

denim-raw-selvedge.low: Raw selvedge denim at low construction under-uses the fabric — the material's character calls for construction detail that reads against its structure.
denim-raw-selvedge.moderate: Raw selvedge denim at moderate construction is the optimal pairing — structured seam work complements the fabric's character without overwhelming it.
denim-raw-selvedge.high: Raw selvedge denim at high construction is technically demanding — the fabric's weight and stiffness drive SMV significantly and require industrial equipment at finishing stages.

denim-conventional.low: Conventional denim at low construction keeps the build fast and cost-stable — appropriate when volume and timeline are the primary constraints.
denim-conventional.moderate: Conventional denim supports moderate construction reliably — it holds seam work cleanly and tolerates finishing detail without handling risk.
denim-conventional.high: Conventional denim at high construction introduces weight and stiffness challenges at complex seam ins — pressing and topstitching protocols must be tightly controlled.
